questions.prompt: Which statement is most consistent with the lecture treatment of physical attractiveness?
options: Attractiveness is entirely objective and universal
Attractiveness is shaped by social norms and systemic factors as well as perceived desirability
Attractiveness matters only after commitment forms
Attractiveness has no relation to first impressions

questions.prompt: The matching phenomenon suggests that people often:
options: Prefer partners who differ maximally in attractiveness
Pair with others who are similar in perceived attractiveness
Ignore attractiveness once similarity is known
Choose partners only based on proximity

questions.prompt: A relationship characterized by high commitment but relatively low passion and intimacy is best described as:
options: Romantic love
Companionate love
Empty love
Consummate love

questions.prompt: Which statement best fits positive illusions in relationships?
options: Accurately noticing every flaw in a partner improves satisfaction
Some idealization of a partner can be associated with relationship satisfaction
Positive illusions require self-deception about abuse
Positive illusions and growth beliefs are identical

questions.prompt: Which response to conflict is active and destructive?
options: Loyalty
Voice
Exit
Neglect

questions.prompt: A partner responds to criticism by saying, "Well, you do the exact same thing," instead of taking responsibility. Gottman would most likely label this:
options: Contempt
Defensiveness
Stonewalling
Responsiveness

questions.prompt: Which infant pattern best fits anxious-avoidant attachment?
options: Clingy exploration and difficulty being soothed
Ignoring or avoiding the caregiver on reunion
Comfortable exploration and easy soothing
Fearful contradictory behaviour

questions.prompt: An anxious-preoccupied adult is most likely to show:
options: Comfort with dependence and independence
Strong desire for closeness combined with insecurity and approval seeking
Preference for emotional distance and self-sufficiency
No concern about rejection

questions.prompt: Which criticism of attachment theory is least consistent with the lecture?
options: It may oversimplify people into fixed types
It often emphasizes early childhood strongly
It was largely developed from one cultural context
It has never been applied to adult relationships at all

questions.prompt: Which statement is most supported regarding 2SLGBTQ+ relationship formation?
options: These relationships always follow traditional heterosexual dating scripts
They may begin as friendships and may not fit prescribed gendered scripts neatly
They avoid all social pressures related to scripts
They are less likely to involve commitment

questions.prompt: Chosen family is especially relevant in the 2SLGBTQ+ lecture because it can:
options: Replace all forms of stress automatically
Serve as a coping resource in the face of minority stress
Eliminate structural discrimination
Prevent all relationship dissolution

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the lecture on transgender experiences in relationships?
options: There is abundant research and few unanswered questions
Transition within a relationship may involve stigma-related challenges, and partner support can be important
Minority stress does not apply
Other relationship findings can never apply

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the best example of stereotyping without discrimination?
options: Assuming a certain group is bad at math
Refusing to rent to someone because of their group
Feeling fear toward an outgroup
Designing a biased policy

questions.prompt: Parental attitudes, religion, and conformity were discussed in lecture as mainly:
options: Cognitive sources of prejudice
Motivational sources of prejudice
Social sources of prejudice
Contact mechanisms

questions.prompt: Which concept best explains why rare or attention-grabbing pairings can create illusory relationships?
options: Distinctiveness in categorization
Secure attachment
Companionate love
Institutional support

questions.prompt: A hiring manager expects women to be less competent in a technical role, gives them less encouragement, and then interprets weaker interviews as confirming the stereotype. This is best understood as:
options: A self-perpetuating stereotype process
Optimal contact
Mutual liking
Loyalty

questions.prompt: Which statement about implicit bias is most accurate according to lecture?
options: It requires deliberate intent
It can operate automatically without introspective access
It predicts all behaviour strongly at the individual level
It only concerns racial attitudes

questions.prompt: The traditional IAT is limited partly because it:
options: Cannot compare categories
Measures only absolute liking for one group
Is relative and usually compares two categories at a time
Cannot use reaction times

questions.prompt: Which healthcare pattern best fits lecture material on implicit gender bias?
options: Women's symptoms always being overinterpreted as cardiac
Delayed diagnosis when psychological symptoms lead providers away from other explanations
No gender differences in diagnostic judgment
Bias appearing only among non-physicians

questions.prompt: In the AI section, the lecture emphasized that large language models can:
options: Be explicitly unbiased and therefore free of biased associations
Still form biased associations even when explicitly trained to avoid bias
Only reflect individual programmer prejudice and nothing else
Never display gendered or racialized patterns

questions.prompt: Which statement is most inconsistent with the social construction view of gender presented in lecture?
options: Gender is shaped by social systems and expectations
Gender relates to, but is not reducible to, biology and reproduction
Gender categories and roles are historically and culturally situated
Gender is fixed entirely by biology and unaffected by social context

questions.prompt: Patriarchy was described in lecture primarily as:
options: A personal attitude held by some men only
A social system that organizes power in gendered ways across institutions
The same thing as benevolent sexism only
A concept unrelated to violence or paid work

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures ambivalent sexism?
options: Only hostility toward women
A combination of hostile and benevolent sexism
Neutrality toward gender roles
Sexism aimed only at men

questions.prompt: A stereotype that portrays a marginalized group as warm but incompetent can help maintain inequality by seeming positive while still limiting status. This is most closely related to:
options: Compensatory stereotypes and paternalistic prejudice
Growth beliefs
Direct structural discrimination
Mere exposure

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the clearest example of direct structural discrimination?
options: A formally stated law prohibiting a group from voting
An informal social norm that advantages some applicants
A neutral rule that has unequal effects
A teacher's unconscious bias

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the clearest example of indirect structural discrimination?
options: A policy explicitly naming a targeted group
A formally announced segregation law
A neutral-seeming policy whose application disadvantages certain groups
A single insulting comment

questions.prompt: In the social ecological model, social norms and institutions affecting whole communities are located mainly at the:
options: Individual level only
Community and societal levels
Dyadic level only
Biological level only

questions.prompt: The invariance hypothesis in social dominance theory concerns the idea that:
options: People high in SDO always prefer equality
Group-based hierarchy shows recurring patterns across contexts
All stereotypes are biologically fixed
Institutional support does not matter

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects a hierarchy-attenuating legitimizing myth?
options: Some groups belong on top
Inequality is natural and necessary
Lower-status groups are just as deserving as higher-status groups
Merit always explains outcomes

questions.prompt: In contact theory, equal status means equal status:
options: In society as a whole, regardless of the interaction
Within the contact situation itself
Only after friendship has already formed
Only when groups are identical

questions.prompt: Why was Robbers Cave important for contact theory?
options: It showed prejudice disappears with exposure alone
It demonstrated both the creation of intergroup hostility through competition and its reduction through cooperation
It disproved the importance of group identity
It showed unequal status is best for cooperation

questions.prompt: Which set lists Allport's optimal conditions most accurately?
options: Equal status, common goals, intergroup cooperation, institutional support
Similarity, attraction, mutual liking, self-esteem
Passion, intimacy, commitment, loyalty
Competition, distinction, anxiety, conformity

questions.prompt: A school restructures group work so students from different backgrounds rely on one another to complete a shared task and teachers actively support the process. This intervention is most directly based on:
options: Stereotype threat
Jigsaw classroom principles from contact theory
Social exchange theory
Benevolent sexism

questions.prompt: One criticism of contact theory is that even successful contact can:
options: Always eliminate structural inequality
Leave larger systems of power unchallenged
Work only under competition
Require outgroup homogeneity

questions.prompt: Which mixed example is matched correctly?
options: "People like us deserve more" -> hierarchy-attenuating myth
"I know she is upset and I want her to feel heard" -> responsiveness
"They all act the same" -> intersectionality
"Women are dangerous liars" -> benevolent sexism
